text.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every application of `AWS Well-Architected Framework` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every WAR review MUST cover all six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability. Skipping a pillar is rejected at sign-off.
rule.rationale: Skipping a pillar creates blind spots; Sustainability is the most-skipped + the new audit surface.

rule.statement: Reviews MUST be captured in the AWS Well-Architected Tool (or equivalent) with question-level answers + risks + improvements per pillar.
rule.rationale: Free-form review docs drift; the WA Tool gives a structured + queryable record.

rule.statement: Each high-risk-finding MUST have a named human owner + due date + remediation ticket. Group-handle ownership forbidden.
rule.rationale: Group-owned findings rot; named owner + ticket forces action.

rule.statement: High-risk findings MUST be remediated ≤ 30 days; medium ≤ 90 days; low ≤ 180 days. Slips MUST be re-reviewed + signed off.
rule.rationale: Findings without SLA slip indefinitely; published SLAs make slippage visible.

rule.statement: Workloads in production MUST be re-reviewed quarterly OR after any major change (new region, new compute pattern, ≥ 30% traffic shift).
rule.rationale: Stale reviews mask drift; quarterly cadence catches it before incidents.

rule.statement: Each WAR answer MUST link to evidence: dashboard URL, runbook page, IaC commit, or ADR. Hunch answers are rejected.
rule.rationale: Unevidenced answers are hopes, not facts; evidence-link forces real verification.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
